Q1: I had a pretty set routine where I'd try to study around 11 hours a week, usually spread across a few evenings. I'd sit down after dinner, go through my readings and notes, and try to keep things consistent. It wasn't super intense, but I figured if I just kept showing up and doing the basics, I'd be fine. I liked having that predictable schedule even if I didn't always dig deep into the material.
Q2: I tried to keep it regular, honestly. I'd set aside Tuesday and Thursday evenings and maybe some time on Sunday afternoons. When deadlines came up, I might add an extra session, but mostly I stuck to my pattern. I'm not the type to cram everything last minute—I just prefer having a routine I can follow without thinking too much about it.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something due or when I remembered to check for announcements. Sometimes it was just habit—like, okay, it's Tuesday, time to see what's on the LMS. I'd also log in if I got an email notification about something new being posted. It wasn't always because I was super eager, just part of what I thought I should be doing.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became pretty automatic. I'd finish dinner, open my laptop, and check the LMS before doing anything else. It just became part of my evening routine, like brushing my teeth. I wouldn't say I was excited about it, but it felt normal and expected. That's kind of how I approach most things—just make it a habit and stick with it.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were the most useful for me since I learn best by reading and writing things down. I'd go through the posted PDFs and take notes in my own words. The discussion boards were okay too because I could see how other people understood the material. Videos were fine but I preferred having something I could read and highlight.
Q6: I kind of ignored the videos a lot of the time, or I'd let them play while doing something else. They just didn't stick with me as well as reading did. Some of the optional practice quizzes I skipped too because I figured if they weren't graded, they weren't essential. Looking back, maybe I should have used them more, but I was just trying to do what was required.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me complete something that was graded, I'd do it properly. I also valued things that were straightforward—like, tell me what to read and I'll read it. Anything that felt vague or extra just didn't make the cut for me.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a bonus that I could skip if I was busy or tired. Required activities had a deadline and consequences, so I made sure to do those. With optional things, I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. It's not that I didn't care, I just prioritized what counted toward my grade.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the PDFs and readings and then study from those on my tablet without being online. I also wrote out notes by hand in a notebook, which helped me remember things better. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS—just me, my notes, and maybe some coffee. That stuff wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd spend an hour or two reviewing my handwritten notes or rereading downloaded materials, and none of that would register on the LMS. I'd also sometimes talk through concepts with my roommate who was in a different major, just to see if I could explain things. All that learning was real, but it was completely invisible online.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd open a PDF once on the LMS, download it, and then never go back to that page again. I'd study from the downloaded file for days or weeks. So my LMS activity might show one quick access, but really I was using that material repeatedly offline. It's just more convenient to have everything saved locally.
Q12: Around midterms, things got harder. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and I started missing more classes than I should have. Even though I wasn't super stressed, I just felt disconnected from the course. It was like I was going through the motions but not really absorbing anything. My attendance dropped and I think that hurt me more than I realized.
Q13: Yeah, when I had a lot of club activities or other assignments piling up, the LMS became less of a priority. I'd still log in and do the minimum, but I wasn't really engaging deeply. Fatigue was a factor too—after a long day, I'd just skim things instead of really working through them. I managed my time okay, but maybe not well enough for this course.
Q14: Honestly, sometimes I'd open the course materials and just stare at them without really processing anything. I'd scroll through readings or let a video play, but my mind was elsewhere. It looked like I was studying, and technically I was logged in, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt like checking a box rather than actually engaging.
Q15: I put off some of the bigger assignments because they felt overwhelming, even when I knew they were important. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd find something else to do first. It wasn't anxiety exactly, just a kind of avoidance. By the time I got to them, I didn't have enough time to do them well.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd log in more frequently as a deadline approached and try to finish whatever was due. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged with some materials at all. It's not the best way to learn, but that's honestly how I operated—deadline equals action.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got comments on assignments, I'd read them, but I didn't always change my approach afterward. If the feedback was positive, I felt okay and kept doing what I was doing. If it was critical, I'd feel a bit deflated but not really motivated to do much differently. I guess I didn't use feedback as well as I could have.
Q18: The course structure was pretty clear, which I appreciated. There were regular modules and due dates, so I knew what to expect each week. That predictability worked well for my routine-based approach. But the structure also meant that if I fell behind, it was hard to catch up because new stuff kept coming. I needed that consistency but also struggled when I missed a beat.
Q19: Having everything organized in weekly modules made it easier to follow along. But some of the assignments were vague about what exactly was expected, which made it harder to know if I was on the right track. Also, the exams covered a lot of material, and I felt like my routine studying wasn't enough to really prepare me. Clearer guidelines would have helped.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to figure things out but not actually producing work. Maybe they're reading and watching videos, trying to understand, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or don't know how to apply what they learned. Or maybe life gets in the way and they run out of time. Activity doesn't always equal output.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me. If someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material from other sources, they might not need to be on the LMS constantly. I did a lot of my work from downloaded files and handwritten notes, so my LMS activity probably looked lower than my actual effort. Performance and logged activity aren't always connected.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and whether you submitted things on time. That's useful for tracking basic participation. But they miss the quality of engagement—like, was I actually thinking about the material or just going through the motions? They also miss all the offline studying and the mental effort that doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just completing tasks. It's about being mentally present, asking questions, and connecting ideas. For me, I thought I was engaged because I had a routine, but looking at my grade, maybe I was just compliant without being truly invested. There's a difference between showing up and really being there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed notes or downloaded files, or the conversations I had with friends about course concepts. It also wouldn't capture that I was going through the motions without really connecting to the material emotionally or intellectually. My routine looked solid on paper, but the depth of my engagement was probably shallower than it appeared.
